3 min read
1 hour ago

--

​I never intended to become an anti-social media crusader. Honestly, I didn’t even think I had a problem.
​Like most of us, my phone was basically an extension of my hand. It was the first thing I reached for before I’d even blinked the sleep out of my eyes, and it was the last thing I stared at before finally passing out. It filled every crack in my day: waiting for the coffee to brew, sitting at a red light, those awkward ten seconds in an elevator. If I was bored, I was scrolling. Period.
​I told myself it was fine. Just checking in, I’d say. Just killing time.
​But then, I saw the screen time report. Six hours.
​I stared at that number on my screen like it was a typo. Six hours a day. That’s a part-time job. I tried to do the math — six hours a day, every day, for years. Where did it go? I couldn’t tell you a single video I watched or post I “liked” that made my life any better. It was just white noise.
​That night, I didn’t delete my accounts — I’m not that brave — but I nuked every single app off my phone. Just for 30 days. An experiment, I told myself.
​The first week was a nightmare.
​My thumb had a muscle memory all its own. I’d unlock my phone to check the weather, and before I knew what was happening, my thumb would automatically tap the empty space where the Instagram icon used to be. It happened over and over. It was like trying to stop biting your nails; you don’t even realize you’re doing it until you’ve already started.
​The first few days, the silence was loud. When you’re used to constant, flashing stimulation, sitting in a quiet room feels like an itch you can’t scratch.
​But then, things shifted.
​About two weeks in, I realized I wasn’t just “not scrolling” — I was actually there. I was eating dinner and I actually tasted my food instead of just inhaling it while watching Reels. I walked my dog and looked at the trees instead of the screen. I finished a book in three days — a book that had been collecting dust on my nightstand for six months.
​The biggest change, though, was in my head. That constant, frantic “need to know” feeling — the fear of missing out, the weird, phantom anxiety about what was happening in other people’s lives — it just evaporated. I stopped comparing my messy, real-time life to everyone else’s highlight reels. I realized that most of the “news” and “trends” I thought were important were just noise.
​By the time the month was up, I had a choice. I didn’t want to go back to being a digital zombie, but I also didn’t want to go off the grid entirely.
​I re-downloaded a few apps, but I changed the terms of service. Notifications are off. The apps are buried in a folder on the last page of my home screen. Now, before I click, I force myself to ask: Why am I doing this? Is this helping me, or am I just bored? If I don’t have a good answer, I put the phone down.
​I’m not saying social media is evil. It’s a tool. But for years, I was the one being used by the tool.
​The most surprising takeaway wasn’t that I became some hyper-productive genius. It was realizing how much of my actual life I had been watching through a tiny, glowing rectangle.
​If your days feel like they’re vanishing into thin air, try it. You don’t need to do a month. Just try deleting the apps for a weekend. You might be shocked at how much room you suddenly have in your head.
​The life you’re looking for isn’t behind the glass. It’s right in front of you.
